required.title: [Read Aloud] A curated collection of picture books
required.author: Jane Yolen, *The Story of Ferdinand* by Munro Leaf, *Sylvester and the Magic Pebble* by William Steig, *Where the Wild Things Are* by Maurice Sendak, *The Snowy Day* by Ezra Jack Keats, *Miss Rumphius* by Barbara Cooney.

required.title: [Read Aloud] Fairy tales from the Brothers Grimm and from world traditions
required.author: Virginia Hamilton, *Tales Our Abuelitas Told* by F. Isabel Campoy and Alma Flor Ada.

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] The Moral of the Story
supplementary.author: Bobby Fong and others

competence_markers.description: The child has been read to daily for the duration of the packet
The child can narrate a story with accurate sequence, character identification, and key details
The child can identify the hero of a story and explain what quality the story values — bravery, kindness, cleverness, honesty, etc.
The child can answer "What does this story want you to think?" with a specific response for at least five different stories
The child has heard stories from at least five different cultural traditions and can name at least one story from a tradition other than their own
The child understands the difference between a true story and a fictional story, and can explain how a fictional story can still teach something true
The child shows emotional engagement with stories — expressing feelings, asking questions, wanting to hear more

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story we just read.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? Who was the hero? What did they do?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does this story want you to think?
narration_prompts.detail: What lesson is the story trying to teach?

narration_prompts.prompt: Who was the hero, and what made them the hero?
narration_prompts.detail: Was it because they were strong, or something else?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a story we read from a faraway place.
narration_prompts.detail: What was different about it from the stories you already knew? What was the same?

narration_prompts.prompt: Which story from this packet is your favorite?
narration_prompts.detail: Why? What did you feel when you heard it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Can a made-up story teach you something true?
narration_prompts.detail: Tell me a story that is not real but teaches a real lesson.

cross_connections.description: While 3.2.5-A1 asks what stories mean and what they teach, 3.2.4-A focuses on the experience of great literature — hearing it, absorbing it, memorizing it. The two packets work in parallel: 3.2.4 builds literary experience; 3.2.5 builds worldview awareness.

The myths, creation stories, and sacred narratives encountered here are the same stories explored in 1.3.1 as expressions of worldview. 3.2.5-A1 introduces them as stories; 1.3.1 asks what they reveal about how different people understand the world.
The child who has heard many stories from many traditions has a rich storehouse of models for their own storytelling in 3.2.3.

The virtues that stories reward — courage, honesty, kindness, cleverness, perseverance — are the same virtues studied in IC1.2. Story is one of the most powerful vehicles for understanding what virtues look like in action.

The myths and folk tales from different cultures encountered here are products of the civilizations studied in 1.1.1. The child who hears Greek myths while learning about ancient Greece encounters a living culture, not a set of facts.

content.why_this_matters:
Every story you hear teaches you something — even when it does not seem like it is teaching. When you hear the story of the Three Little Pigs, you learn that planning ahead matters and laziness has consequences. When you hear the story of the Good Samaritan, you learn that kindness crosses boundaries. When you hear the story of Anansi the Spider, you learn that cleverness can beat strength. You may not notice these lessons while you are hearing the story. That is what makes stories so powerful — they teach you things by putting you inside someone else's experience, so the lesson feels like something that happened to you instead of something someone told you.

This packet is about building the foundation for a lifetime of understanding how stories work. Right now, the most important thing is that you hear many, many stories — read to you by someone who loves them. Picture books, fairy tales, myths, folk tales from around the world. You will hear stories from your own culture and stories from cultures very different from yours. You will hear stories where the hero is brave and stories where the hero is clever. You will hear stories where good wins and stories where the ending is complicated.

As you hear these stories, you will start to notice something: every story has an opinion about the world. Every story thinks something is important. And the more stories you hear, the better you will get at noticing what each story is telling you about how the world works, what matters, and what kind of person you should be.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Daily read-aloud habit** — the child is read to every day, building the routine that makes literary immersion possible
2. **Story absorption** — the child develops the ability to follow a narrative, remember characters and events, and retell what they heard
3. **Moral awareness through story** — the child begins to notice that stories teach lessons about how to live
4. **Exposure to diverse narrative traditions** — the child hears stories from multiple cultures, encountering different values and assumptions
5. **The question "what does this story want me to think?"** — the earliest form of worldview awareness
6. **Emotional engagement with characters** — the child feels what characters feel, building empathy and moral imagination
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Stories Are Teachers

When someone reads you a story, they are not just keeping you entertained. They are giving you a way to think about the world. Every story — every single one — has something it thinks is important. Sometimes the lesson is obvious: "The Tortoise and the Hare" thinks that slow and steady wins. Sometimes the lesson is hidden: *Where the Wild Things Are* is about a boy who is angry, explores that anger, and then chooses to come home — the story thinks that even wild feelings can be explored safely and that home is where you are loved.

You do not need to find the "right answer" about what a story means. What matters is that you start noticing: What does this story care about? What does it think is important?

### Part 2: Heroes Tell You What a Story Values

Every story has a hero — the character the story wants you to care about most. What the hero does, what they value, what they are rewarded for — all of this tells you what the story thinks is important.

In some stories, the hero is brave. They face danger and do not run away. These stories value courage. In other stories, the hero is kind. They help someone no one else will help. These stories value compassion. In trickster tales — stories about characters like Anansi the Spider or Coyote — the hero is clever. They outsmart bigger, stronger opponents. These stories value wit and resourcefulness.

When you hear a story, notice: Who is the hero? What makes them the hero? It is usually not because they are the strongest or the prettiest. It is because they have a quality the story thinks matters most.

### Part 3: Different Stories, Different Lessons

Stories from different places and different times sometimes teach different things. A Greek myth might teach you that the gods punish pride — that you should never think you are greater than the gods. A Norse myth might teach you that even the gods are doomed, and what matters is fighting bravely even when you know you will lose. A fairy tale from West Africa might teach you that cleverness is the most valuable gift. A parable from the Bible might teach you that kindness to strangers is what God asks of you.

None of these lessons are wrong. They are different — because the people who told these stories lived in different places, faced different challenges, and believed different things about the world. Hearing stories from many different traditions helps you understand that there are many ways to think about what matters in life. You do not have to agree with all of them. But you should hear them, because each one shows you something true about how some people, somewhere, understood the world.

### Part 4: "What Does This Story Want Me to Think?"

This is the most important question you will learn in this packet. After you hear a story, ask yourself: What does this story want me to think? What does it want me to believe about the world?

*The Three Little Pigs* wants you to think that planning ahead and doing things right matters more than doing things quickly.

*The Boy Who Cried Wolf* wants you to think that lying destroys trust, and once trust is gone, no one will believe you even when you tell the truth.

*The Giving Tree* wants you to think — well, people disagree about this one. Some people think it is a beautiful story about love and generosity. Other people think it is a troubling story about a tree that gives everything and a boy who takes everything without gratitude. That disagreement is important. It means the story is asking a real question, not just giving you an easy answer.

You will get better at this question over time. Right now, the goal is simply to start asking it.

### Part 5: Stories That Are True and Stories That Are Made Up

Some stories are true — they really happened. Some stories are made up — they came from someone's imagination. But here is something important to understand: a story can be made up and still teach you something true.

"The Boy Who Cried Wolf" never really happened. There was no actual boy who cried wolf. But the lesson — that lying destroys trust — is absolutely true. You have probably experienced it yourself. The story uses a made-up situation to teach a real lesson.

Myths are stories that ancient people told about their gods and heroes. Did Zeus really throw lightning bolts? Probably not. But the Greek myths teach real things about pride, jealousy, courage, and consequences. The story of Icarus — who flew too close to the sun because he would not listen to his father — is made up. But the lesson about the danger of ignoring wise advice is real.

Understanding that stories can be fictional and still true is one of the most important ideas you will ever learn about how stories work.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Read-Aloud [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read to the child every day — a minimum of 15-20 minutes per session. Use picture books, fairy tales, myths, and folk tales from the resource list. After each story, the child narrates what they heard and discusses what the story was about — not just what happened, but what it meant.

### Study 2: "What Does This Story Want Me to Think?" Practice [Ongoing]
After every story, ask: "What does this story want you to think?" The child practices identifying the moral or lesson. Accept all reasonable answers — the goal is the habit of asking, not arriving at a single correct interpretation. Over time, the child's answers will become more specific and thoughtful.

### Study 3: Hero Identification [Early-to-Mid Packet]
For each story read, the child identifies: Who is the hero? What makes them the hero? What quality does the story value most — bravery, kindness, cleverness, honesty, something else? Keep a running list: story name, hero, and the quality the story values. Review the list together: Do all stories value the same thing? What are the differences?

### Study 4: Stories from Around the World [Mid-Packet]
Read at least five stories from five different cultural traditions (one each from: Western European, African, Asian, Indigenous American, and Middle Eastern or biblical traditions). For each, discuss: What does this story think is important? Is it the same as or different from the other stories we have read? What is similar? What is different?

### Study 5: A Story That Made You Feel Something [Late Packet]
The child picks the story that made them feel the strongest emotion during this packet — happy, sad, scared, angry, amazed. They narrate the story and explain what they felt and why. Discussion: Why did the story make you feel that way? What did the author do to make you feel it? This is the earliest form of understanding how stories work on us emotionally.

### Study 6: True and Made Up [Late Packet]
The child sorts a collection of familiar stories into three categories: true stories (really happened), made-up stories (fictional), and made-up stories that teach something true. Discuss: Can a made-up story teach you something real? Can a true story be told in a way that teaches the wrong lesson? These questions do not need definitive answers at this age — they need to be asked.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Story Engagement
**Developing well:** The child is eager for story time. They listen attentively, react emotionally, ask questions, request favorites again, and narrate with detail and enthusiasm.

**Developing slowly:** The child is restless, disengaged, or indifferent during reading. Stories do not generate questions or emotional responses.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the reading happening at a good time (alert, not tired)? Is the environment comfortable and distraction-free? Are the stories at the right level — engaging enough to hold attention, not too complex to follow?

### 2. Moral Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can answer "What does this story want you to think?" with a specific response. Their answers may be simple ("Be honest" or "Don't be mean") but they show engagement with the story's meaning, not just its plot.

**Developing slowly:** The child can only retell events. "What does the story want you to think?" produces confusion or a blank stare.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking the question consistently after readings? Are you modeling the kind of answer you are looking for — sharing your own interpretation before asking for theirs? Some children need to hear several examples before they understand what the question is asking.

### 3. Diverse Tradition Exposure
**Developing well:** The child can name stories from different traditions and has noticed that different stories value different things.

**Developing slowly:** The reading has been limited to one tradition (typically Western European fairy tales). The child has no exposure to non-Western storytelling.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you deliberately included stories from at least five cultural traditions? If not, the gap is in the curriculum, not the child.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Moralizing Instead of Discussing
"The lesson of this story is that you should always be honest." This kills the conversation. Instead: "What do you think this story wants you to learn?" Let the child discover. If they get somewhere surprising, explore it with them.

### Reading Only Familiar Stories
It is comfortable to read the stories you grew up with. Push beyond that. Seek out folk tales from cultures you are not familiar with. The child's worldview is being formed right now — the breadth of their story exposure shapes the breadth of their imagination.

### Underestimating the Child
Young children can handle stories with real moral complexity — the Grimm fairy tales, Aesop's harsher fables, biblical narratives where the heroes are flawed. Do not assume the child cannot handle darkness or ambiguity. They can, and they need to. A diet of only cheerful, simple stories produces a child who is unprepared for a world that is neither cheerful nor simple.

### Skipping Narration
After every story, the child should narrate — retell what happened and what it meant. Skipping this step reduces the reading to passive entertainment. Narration transforms it into active learning.
